topic: A teacher receives an unexpected gift from a former student
raw_output:
[story 1]

Professor Eleanor Vance smoothed the lapel of her tweed jacket, a gesture that felt stiff and unnecessary. The small, intricately carved wooden bird sat on her desk, a stark contrast to the usual stack of ungraded papers. It was a gift, wrapped in faded blue paper, addressed simply to ‘E.V.’ From the envelope, she recognized the familiar, looping script of Liam, a student she’d mentored during her final year. A tight knot formed in Eleanor’s chest, a sudden, unwelcome heat rising to her cheeks. She picked up the bird; the wood felt cool and smooth beneath her fingertips. She quickly tucked it into a drawer, avoiding eye contact with the empty room, acutely aware of the silence amplifying the thumping in her own ears.

[story 2]

"Oh, for heaven's sake," Marcus muttered under his breath, his jaw tight as he stared at the brightly colored, slightly crumpled drawing resting on his mahogany desk. It was a meticulously rendered sketch of the school library, complete with tiny, almost invisible figures of students reading. The card accompanying it was brief, signed only with a flourish. When Dr. Albright presented it—a gesture so personal, so out of place—Marcus felt a strange pressure build behind his eyes. He cleared his throat, the sound rougher than intended. "It's... thoughtful, Mr. Albright. Truly." He focused intensely on the grain of the wood on his desk, willing himself to remain perfectly still, lest any tremor betray the sudden churning in his stomach.

[story 3]

Sarah felt the weight of the small, silver locket in her palm. It was delicate, undeniably expensive, and bore a tiny engraving of a constellation. It had arrived this morning, left discreetly on her doorstep. When she opened the package, the sheer unexpectedness of it made her hand tremble slightly. She looked at the note—a simple, heartfelt message from David, a student from freshman orientation. Sarah gripped the locket so hard her knuckles whitened. She couldn't meet the eyes of the visitor, feeling suddenly exposed, as if the quiet dignity she usually wore had slipped away, leaving her feeling strangely small and adrift in the sudden spotlight.
